question: Explain the primary difference between a stack and a queue data structure. Provide a real-world example for each to illustrate their use.
expectedPoints: A stack is a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) structure where the last element added is the first one to be removed.
A queue is a First-In, First-Out (FIFO) structure where the first element added is the first one to be removed.
Real-world example for a stack: the 'undo' functionality in an application, where the last action performed is the first one undone.
Real-world example for a queue: a printer job queue, where documents are printed in the order they were received.

question: Compare and contrast the time complexities for insertion, deletion, and access operations in an array versus a linked list. In which scenarios would you choose one over the other?
expectedPoints: Arrays provide O(1) time complexity for access by index and O(n) for insertion/deletion at the beginning/middle due to the need to shift elements.
Linked lists provide O(1) time complexity for insertion/deletion at the head (or tail, if doubly linked) but O(n) for access by index as traversal is required.
An array is preferable when frequent random access is needed and the size is relatively fixed.
A linked list is preferable when frequent insertions and deletions are performed, especially at the beginning, and when the size is dynamic.

question: Describe the process of balancing an AVL tree after an insertion operation that causes an imbalance. Walk through the necessary rotations (LL, RR, LR, RL) with a brief explanation of when each is applied.
expectedPoints: An AVL tree uses rotations to maintain a balance factor (height difference of left and right subtrees) of -1, 0, or 1 for every node.
LL Rotation (Right Rotation): Applied when a node becomes unbalanced due to an insertion in the left subtree of its left child.
RR Rotation (Left Rotation): Applied when a node becomes unbalanced due to an insertion in the right subtree of its right child.

LR Rotation (Left-Right Rotation): Applied when a node becomes unbalanced due to an insertion in the right subtree of its left child. Requires a left rotation on the left child followed by a right rotation on the unbalanced node.

RL Rotation (Right-Left Rotation): Applied when a node becomes unbalanced due to an insertion in the left subtree of its right child. Requires a right rotation on the right child followed by a left rotation on the unbalanced node.
